claims.claim: Generic startup guidance recommends SOM (not SAM) capture benchmarks of 1% of SAM in Year 1, 3% in Year 2, 5% in Year 3 — no equivalent SAM-as-%-of-TAM benchmark is given.
claims.sourceTitle: Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)
claims.excerpt: SOM benchmarks that won't get you laughed out of the room: 1% of SAM in Year 1, 3% in Year 2, 5% in Year 3.

claims.claim: Cross-check heuristic: top-down and bottom-up market estimates should land within 2x of each other to be considered healthy.
claims.sourceTitle: Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)
claims.excerpt: Cross-check rule of thumb: if your top-down and bottom-up estimates land within 2x of each other, your assumptions are healthy. If they're 10x apart, something's broken.

claims.claim: Alternative version of the same heuristic uses a 3x tolerance band instead of 2x.
claims.sourceTitle: Serviceable Addressable Market: How to Calculate SAM
claims.excerpt: validate by checking that your top-down and bottom-up estimates stay within 3x of each other

claims.claim: SAM is defined generically as the realistically reachable portion of TAM given current product/go-to-market, not a fixed universal ratio.
claims.excerpt: SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market) is the portion of the market you can realistically serve with your current product and go-to-market constraints—typically defined by who you sell to, where you can sell, and what you can actually support and deliver.

claims.claim: Compliance/regulatory requirements are explicitly listed as one of several qualitative factors that shrink TAM into SAM, alongside geography and product readiness — not quantified into a single percentage.
claims.excerpt: Compliance and risk: regulatory requirements (SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR), procurement realities.

claims.claim: A free TAM/SAM/SOM calculator tool leaves the SAM 'segment %' input blank/zero by default, requiring the user to supply their own percentage rather than offering an industry benchmark.
claims.sourceTitle: TAM SAM SOM Calculator 2026 | Free Market Size Analysis | IdeaProof

claims.claim: Generic TAM/SAM/SOM frameworks are described as unreliable specifically for technical/industrial B2B hardware markets because they ignore application-specific requirements and buyer complexity.
claims.sourceTitle: Niche B2B Market Sizing Guide: TAM SAM SOM Framework for Technical Sales Team - GrowthBeaver
claims.excerpt: Most TAM calculators give you fantasy numbers. Here's why yours probably overestimate by 300%.

claims.claim: Generic B2B market sizing errors (not specific to hardware/serviceable share) are estimated to cost businesses up to $15M annually.
claims.sourceTitle: Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)
claims.excerpt: Inaccurate addressable market estimates cost businesses up to $15M annually.

claims.claim: EU Regulation 2019/424 establishes ecodesign requirements gating the placing on the market and putting into service of servers and online data storage products.
claims.excerpt: This Regulation establishes ecodesign requirements for the placing on the market and putting into service of servers and online data storage products.

claims.claim: From 1 January 2023, minimum PSU efficiency for multi-output units must be at least 90%/94%/91% at 20%/50%/100% load, and power factor at least 0.95 at 50% load — a hard compliance gate for server hardware entering the EU market.
claims.excerpt: Minimum PSU efficiency Minimum power factor % of rated load 10 % 20 % 50 % 100 % 50 % Multi output — 90 % 94 % 91 % 0,95

claims.claim: The EU's Energy Efficiency Directive imposes a collective energy consumption reduction target of at least 11.7% by 2030 versus 2020 projections, with specific disclosure/waste-heat-reuse requirements on datacenter operators.
claims.sourceTitle: The EU's Energy Efficiency Directive and Its Impact on Datacenters
claims.excerpt: Imposes on Member States a collective energy consumption reduction target of at least 11,7% in 2030 compared to the projections of the EU reference scenario for 2020

claims.claim: EN 50600 is a holistic European standard covering data center planning, construction, and operation (power, cooling, security) — a facility-level certification distinct from product-level ecodesign rules.
claims.sourceTitle: EN 50600 – European standard for data centers | TÜV NORD
claims.excerpt: The European standard EN 50600 provides comprehensive guidelines for the planning, construction, and operation of data centers, focusing on power supply, air conditioning, and security infrastructure.

claims.claim: A separate EU delegated regulation (2024/1364) establishes the first phase of a common Union rating scheme for data centres, adding another regulatory layer beyond ecodesign/EN 50600.
claims.excerpt: Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/1364 of 14 March 2024 on the first phase of the establishment of a common Union rating scheme for data centres

claims.claim: A market-entry consultancy lists the full certification/compliance stack required for EU hardware market entry as WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, FCC, ROHS plus customs barriers (EORI, custom-forms) — broader than the cert set named in the node's derivation.
claims.excerpt:
Necessary certifications (WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, FCC, ROHS)
- Market entry barriers (EORI, custom-forms)

tensions.text: The node's derivation asserts 'No public SAM/TAM benchmark for B2B hardware exists' — this search confirms the absence directly: all SAM/TAM ratio content found is generic startup/SaaS methodology (2x-3x cross-check rules, 1%/3%/5% SOM capture curves), none specific to B2B hardware or quantifying a serviceable-share number, and one calculator even defaults the SAM segment input to 0% rather than a benchmark.

The node's rationale bundles EN 50600, ecodesign 2019/424, and CE/TÜV together as one 'EU cert stack' justifying <1.0 serviceable share, but the search results show these are distinct regulatory layers operating at different levels (product ecodesign vs. facility construction/operation standard vs. a newer 2024 data-centre rating scheme vs. general product compliance like CE/WEEE/RoHS/FCC) — the stack is broader and more fragmented than the single justification implies, and none of them individually implies a specific percentage haircut.

Industry guidance repeatedly warns that top-down vs. bottom-up market-size estimates should be validated to stay within 2x-3x of each other, yet the node has no bottom-up estimate to cross-check its top-down 0.55 assumption against, leaving the sensitivity range (0.45-0.65) methodologically unvalidated by this standard.

absences: No public SAM-as-%-of-TAM benchmark specific to B2B hardware, servers, or data center equipment vendors was found — all numeric SAM/SOM benchmarks located are generic SaaS/B2B software heuristics (e.g., 1%/3%/5% SOM capture, 2x-3x top-down/bottom-up tolerance).

No bottom-up channel-coverage model (named accounts × reachable share) or any sourced quantification tying EU certification/regulatory requirements to a specific serviceable-market percentage was found.

No quantitative estimate of how much of the addressable market is excluded specifically by EN 50600, ecodesign 2019/424, or CE/TÜV certification requirements — only qualitative existence and content of the standards themselves.
No data-center or server-hardware-specific TAM/SAM/SOM case study or industry report with a stated serviceable ratio was returned despite the query targeting B2B hardware benchmarks.
